mnemonics.title: MOPSOW Priority Order
mnemonics.content: M-Must have (Critical), O-Other (Should have), S-Should have, C-Could have, W-Won't have, O-Oh well (out of scope)

mnemonics.title: Stakeholder Analysis Steps
mnemonics.content: I-P-I: Identify stakeholders → Power/Interest analysis → Engagement strategy planning

acronyms.expansion: Best Alternative To a Negotiated Agreement
acronyms.usage: Used to determine your walk-away point in negotiations

acronyms.expansion: Must have, Should have, Could have, Won't have
Kano (Named after Professor Noriaki Kano) - Basic, Performance, Attractive
acronyms.usage: Model for categorizing requirements by satisfaction impact
Links requirements to design, code, and test artifacts
Quality attributes like performance, security, usability

visualTips.visualization: Draw a 2x2 matrix with Power (Y-axis) vs Interest (X-axis). Place stakeholders: High/High in top-right (Manage Closely), High/Low in top-left (Keep Satisfied), Low/High in bottom-right (Keep Informed), Low/Low in bottom-left (Monitor).

Draw a curve showing: Basic needs at bottom flat (dissatisfaction when missing), Performance in diagonal (linear satisfaction), Delighters at top curve (exponential satisfaction when present). Think of it like a temperature graph - basic is base temperature, performance is heating/cooling, delighters are bonus heat.
Visualize three concentric circles: Inner = Walk-away point (BATNA), Middle = Negotiation zone (acceptable range), Outer = Target (ideal outcome). Agreement happens in the middle zone.

analogies.analogy: Like planning a family vacation where dad wants adventure, mom wants relaxation, kids want theme parks - negotiation finds a destination satisfying everyone (maybe a resort near a theme park).
Instead of splitting a pizza fixed at 8 slices, find ways to make a 12-slice pizza so everyone gets more than the original 4 slices.
Like going to a grocery store without a list - you keep adding items until your cart is full and budget exceeded, losing focus on what you originally needed.
Like an emergency room doctor assessing patients - quickly categorize who needs immediate attention, who can wait, and who doesn't need care at all.
Hard requirements are like the foundation of a house (non-negotiable). Soft requirements are like paint colors - you discuss and choose but can compromise.
